skill.name: shader-programming-glsl
skill.description: Expert guide for writing efficient GLSL shaders (Vertex/Fragment) for web and game engines, covering syntax, uniforms, and common effects.
skill.summary: Comprehensive GLSL shader programming guide for creating GPU-accelerated visual effects in WebGL and game engines.
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: All 33 static analysis findings are false positives. The SKILL.md file is educational documentation about GLSL shader programming. Backtick-quoted text represents GLSL code examples in markdown format, not shell command execution. C2 keywords and system reconnaissance patterns are GLSL built-in variables (gl_Position, gl_FragColor, varying) and shader language syntax. No actual security risks detected.

content.value_statement: Create stunning visual effects and optimize graphics rendering with GPU shaders. This guide teaches GLSL syntax, vertex and fragment shaders, and shader math for real-time graphics.
actual_capabilities: Explain GLSL shader syntax including uniforms, varyings, and built-in variables
Generate vertex and fragment shader code for WebGL and game engines
Create procedural effects like raymarching, SDF rendering, and post-processing
Optimize shader performance by reducing branching and using vector operations
Debug common shader issues like black screens and incorrect coordinate transforms
Teach swizzling and vector math for efficient GPU code
limitations: Provides code guidance only; cannot execute or compile shaders
Shader examples require a WebGL context or game engine to render
Performance optimization depends on target GPU hardware
Cannot preview shader output visually within chat

use_cases.title: Web Developer Creating Interactive Visuals
use_cases.description: Build custom shaders for Three.js projects, including animated backgrounds, particle systems, and post-processing effects for websites.

use_cases.title: Game Developer Implementing Custom Effects
use_cases.description: Write shaders for Unity or Unreal Engine to create unique visual styles, terrain generation, and real-time lighting effects.

use_cases.title: Creative Coder Exploring Generative Art
use_cases.description: Experiment with procedural generation using SDFs, raymarching, and fragment shader techniques for digital art installations.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Fragment Shader Setup
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me write a basic GLSL fragment shader that creates a color gradient based on UV coordinates for use in Three.js.

prompt_templates.title: Vertex Shader with Transform
prompt_templates.prompt: Generate a vertex shader that applies a wave deformation to a mesh using a time uniform and sin/cos functions.

prompt_templates.title: Raymarching SDF Scene
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a raymarching fragment shader with multiple SDF shapes (sphere and box) combined using smooth union operations.

prompt_templates.title: Post-Processing Effect
prompt_templates.prompt: Write a bloom post-processing shader that extracts bright pixels, applies Gaussian blur, and composites back over the original image.

output_examples.input: Create a shader that makes a pulsing glow effect centered on the screen
output_examples.output: A fragment shader using smoothstep and time-based sin functions to create radial gradient animation with color interpolation from center to edges

output_examples.input: How do I optimize this shader loop that runs 100 iterations?
output_examples.output: Recommendations to reduce iteration count, use early termination, precompute constants on CPU, and replace branching with step/mix functions for better GPU performance

best_practices: Use mix() for interpolation and step()/smoothstep() for thresholds instead of if-else branching
Precompute constant values on CPU and pass as uniforms rather than calculating in shader
Pack related data into vec4 to minimize memory bandwidth and leverage GPU vector units
anti_patterns: Heavy conditional branching inside loops reduces GPU parallelism and causes thread divergence
Computing static values per-fragment instead of passing precomputed uniforms from CPU
Unnecessary precision (using highp when mediump suffices) wastes bandwidth and power on mobile

faq.question: What is the difference between vertex and fragment shaders?
faq.answer: Vertex shaders transform 3D vertex positions to 2D screen coordinates. Fragment shaders determine the final color of each pixel. Vertex shaders run once per vertex; fragment shaders run once per pixel.

faq.question: Why is my shader showing a black screen?
faq.answer: Check that gl_Position.w equals 1.0, verify uniforms are set from your host application, ensure UV coordinates are in the 0-1 range, and confirm your shader compiled without errors.

faq.question: What does swizzling mean in GLSL?
faq.answer: Swizzling lets you reorder or select vector components using suffixes like .xyzw, .rgba, or .stpq. For example, color.zyx reverses the RGB channels of a vec3.

faq.question: How do I pass data from vertex to fragment shader?
faq.answer: Use the varying keyword. Declare a varying in both shaders, assign it in the vertex shader, and read it in the fragment shader. Values are interpolated across the primitive.

faq.question: What is raymarching and when should I use it?
faq.answer: Raymarching renders 3D scenes by stepping along rays using signed distance functions. Use it for procedural geometry, organic shapes, and effects difficult with traditional rasterization.

faq.question: How can I improve shader performance on mobile devices?
faq.answer: Use mediump precision when possible, minimize texture lookups, reduce loop iterations, avoid dynamic branching, and precompute complex calculations on the CPU.
